coredata.dc:title: Ignition of photoluminescent intensity of quenched MoS2 quantum dots tetracycline mixture by levofloxacin via photoinduced electron transfer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Sri, Smriti
Singh, Udisha
Kumar, Rahul
Lakshmi, G.B.V.S.
Solanki, Pratima R.
coredata.dc:description: A novel switchable signalling sensor based on molybdenum disulphide quantum dots (MoS2qds) have been developed as a fluorescent chemo sensor with high selectivity towards tetracycline (Tc) and levofloxacin (Levo). Tc upon interacting with MoS2qds forms a mixture which quenches the photoluminescent intensity of MoS2qds because of the dynamic quenching. A noteworthy increase in the fluorescence was observed when the MoS2qds-Tc mixture was complexed with Levo. This enhancement in the fluorescence is governed by a photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process in which the energy level ordering of the excited states is changed by the formation of complex of Levo with MoS2qds-Tc mixture due to the rotation of chromophore in Levo. The fluorescence recovery efficiency of Levo is 283%. The proposed sensor was used to analyse spiked samples of water, sewage and milk, the results found are in accordance with the experimental trend.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2qds
Optical sensor
Label-free sensor
Tetracycline hydrochloride
Levofloxacin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent chemo sensing of Tc and levo

The work done here is supported by Department of Biotechnology , New Delhi, India –Indo-Russia project ( DBT /IC-2/Indo-Russia/2017–19/02). One of the authors, Smriti Sri is thankful to UGC for providing financial support through SRF. GBVSL is thankful to DST, India funding through women scientist project (SR/WOS-A/PM-108/2016).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illumination of MoS2qds synthesis and its application as a fluorescence chemo sensor (b) UV/Vis spectra of MoS2qds, the inset shows Tauc plot for band gap energy calculation of 3.71 ​eV (c) fluorescence spectra of MoS2qds at different excitation wavelength (Ex. 290–330 ​nm), the inset shows the peak fitted graph (d) FTIR spectrum of MoS2qds.
(a) TEM images at a scale of 20 ​nm and (b) Histogram of size distribution of MoS2qds.

(a) Fluorescence sensing of Tc, inset shows the peak fit (b) Calibration plot between the Tc Concentrations vs. fluorescence intensity, inset shows the linearity plot (c) Fluorescence sensing of Levo, inset shows the peak fit and (d) Calibration plot between the Levo concentrations vs. fluorescence intensity, inset shows the linearity plot.

(a) Fluorescent chemo-sensor with high selectivity towards Tc and Levo (b) Dynamic quenching after adding Tc to MoS2qds (c) proposed PET mechanism for Levo sensing (d) interaction of Levo with MoS2qds-Tc mixture.
MoS2qds detected Tc and Levo in spiked samples of (a) Milk, (b) Sewage water and (c) Tap water.
Interference study with respect to few other antibiotics and components of milk (Erythro and Genta stands for erythromycin and gentamycin, respectively).
Shows different biosensor developed for Tc and Levo detection.

head.ce:title: Ignition of photoluminescent intensity of quenched MoS2 quantum dots tetracycline mixture by levofloxacin via photoinduced electron transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Special Centre for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, 110067, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Special Centre for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, 110067, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel switchable signalling sensor based on molybdenum disulphide quantum dots (MoS2qds) have been developed as a fluorescent chemo sensor with high selectivity towards tetracycline (Tc) and levofloxacin (Levo). Tc upon interacting with MoS2qds forms a mixture which quenches the photoluminescent intensity of MoS2qds because of the dynamic quenching. A noteworthy increase in the fluorescence was observed when the MoS2qds-Tc mixture was complexed with Levo. This enhancement in the fluorescence is governed by a photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process in which the energy level ordering of the excited states is changed by the formation of complex of Levo with MoS2qds-Tc mixture due to the rotation of chromophore in Levo. The fluorescence recovery efficiency of Levo is 283%. The proposed sensor was used to analyse spiked samples of water, sewage and milk, the results found are in accordance with the experimental trend.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing usage of antibiotics has resulted in the undesirable accumulation in the environment causing a threat to the lives of animals as well as humans. Tetracycline (Tc) antibiotics are one of the broad-spectrum antibiotics primarily used for veterinary process because of its conducive oral absorption, antibacterial properties, low toxicity and cost-effectiveness [1]. However, abusing Tc could lead to its accumulation in meat products, honey and milk which may trigger allergic response upon consumption. Even worse it can induce antibiotic resistance in bacteria, a global concern [2–4]. In the wake of potential toxicity on human health the maximal residual limit of Tc has been strictly restricted in many countries. European Union (EU) has set Mean residual limits (MRLs) for TCs at 0.3 ​mg ​kg−1 in liver, 0.1 ​mg ​kg−1 in milk or muscle tissues, and 0.01 ​mg ​kg−1 in honey. The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) also has similar MRLs: 2 ​mg ​kg−1 in muscle, 6 ​mg ​kg−1 in liver, and 0.4 ​mg ​kg−1 in milk [5].

Besides this, Fluoroquinolones are the broad-spectrum antibiotics effective against a wide range of bacterial species. These drugs are commonly used to treat a variety of diseases such as respiratory and urinary tract infections. Levofloxacin (Levo) is one of the third generations of fluoroquinolones. It is widely used in livestock. Levo is often considered harmless but they exhibit serious side effects as they interfere with the binding of neuroreceptors y-aminobutyric acid (GABA) to the GABA receptors, leading to central nervous system excitation thereby causing peripheral neuropathy. Other reported adverse effects of Levo treatment are heart diseases, tendon damage, pseudomembranous colitis, muscle wasting, Stevens-Johnson syndrome and acute psychosis as well [6–9]. MRLs value for different fluoroquinolones vary from 100 to 50 ​μg/l [10].

Various analytical methods for the detection of antibiotics including Tc have been developed such as high performance liquid chromatography, mass spectroscopy, immunoassays and microbial assay [11]. These are reliable but require more time and well-trained professionals. Fortunately, the highly concerned fluorescence method has many advantages of simple operation, fast detection and high reliability, which endow it with the proper alternative in analysis. Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), a transition metal dichalcogenides have gained enormous interest due to their excellent electrical conductivity and catalytsis poperty [12]. When MoS2 is reduced to 0D, the quantum dots (Qds) exhibits brilliant photoluminiscent properties [13]. Qds are semiconductor nanocrystals and they show enhanced and excellent optoelectronic properties differing from bulk of the same material due to their quantum confinement and edge effect [14]. The band gap energy (Eg) also increases due to quantum confinement effect. The size of MoS2qds is less than 10 ​nm (0D) and have large Eg (3.96 ​eV) as compared to 2D nanosheets (Eg of 1.89 ​eV) [15]. They have high edge to volume ratio and high electron transport than the other MoS2 nanostructures. They are stable, biocompatible, least toxic, abundant in nature and therefore they are used in several applications like hydrogen evolution reaction, lithium storage, bio-imaging, sensing and photocatalyst [16].

Optical sensors are gaining popularity due to ease of use, fast processing at low cost, low detection limit and high sensitivity. Tc is detected using optical techniques as reported in literature [17–19]. Mostly electrochemical biosensors are reported for the Levo and Tc detection [9,20,21] and a few optical based sensors for Levo and Tc. Table 1 shows the comparative analytical performance of reported optical sensors in literature. Shanin et al., have optically detected Levo using fluorescence polarization immunoassay [22]. Recently, Li et al., reported a ratio metric fluorescence sensor for detection of Levo using N,S functionalised carbon dots (CDs) [23]. Tan et al., have reported the fluorescence sensor for oxytetracycline sensor based on graphene oxide (GO) hydrogel [24]. A magnetic nanoparticles system (Fe3O4 MNPs) was reported for Tc detection based on colorimetric technique [25]. He et al., developed an unlabelled optical sensing platform for Tc detection using an aptamer-gold nanoparticle (AuNP) [26]. Chen et al., developed another optical, a novel electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor based on Ru (bpy)3(2+)− doped silica nanoparticles [27]. Wang et al., have previously synthesized MoS2qds and used them for the detection of a single antibiotic Tc [28]. Whereas, present study reports the simultaneous detection of two antibiotics Tc and Levo using the same MoS2qds, as the two antibiotics interacts differently as Tc acts as quencher and Levo ignites the fluorescence intensity of quenched MoS2qds-Tc mixture.

In this manuscript, MoS2qds were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method using sodium molybdate and l-cysteine as precursor material for Mo and S, respectively [13]. The synthesized MoS2qds have good optical properties. The MoS2qds show excitation dependent fluorescence behaviour (Fig. 1 (c)). The MoS2qds are very small in size with a narrow size distribution as observed by TEM. These MoS2qds were used for the fabrication of a novel “Off-on” type of PET sensor for Levo. On adding Tc, the fluorescence intensity decreases linearly due to the formation of Tc-MoS2qds mixture. Upon the addition of Levo, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture increased linearly due to the interaction between the intermediate complex and Levo, because of ligand exchange between the mixture and Levo.

The main exciting feature of this optical chemo sensor is rapid and label free detection of Tc and Levo using one single MoS2qds. Moreover, the optical sensor can detect a wide range of antibiotic concentrations. Further, this optical sensor is validated by interference study and spiked sample study. The optimal performance of the proposed chemo sensor is corroborated in milk, tap water and sewage water samples under optimal conditions. The results showed that the optical sensor can be easily upgraded to the real sample analysis without any additional modulation. It also suggested that the present method is easily scalable from bench side to bedside.
Materials details and methods have been explained in detail in the Supplementary file.

200 ​μL of MoS2qds in 1700 ​μL of deionised water were used for all the fluorescence spectroscopic studies at the excitation wavelength of 310 ​nm 100 ​μL of different concentrations of antibiotics (Tc and Levo) were added to this solution and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. The fluorescence studies were done at a slit width of 5 in fluorescence spectrophotometer. All the sensing studies were performed more than thrice under similar condition at room temperature.

Interference studies were done with other antibiotics (erythromycin, ofloxacin, gentamycin and ampicillin) having fixed concentration of 100 ​mM. Besides this, interfering components of milk (casein, citric acid, ascorbic acid, urea, BSA) were also studied with fixed concentration of 1 ​mM.

Fluorescence life-time studies were done with three different concentrations of MoS2qds-Tc and MoS2qds-Tc-Levo. However, the volumes of the MoS2qds, Tc and Levo were taken according to the assay i.e., 200 ​μL of MoS2qds in 1700 ​μL of deionised water and 100 ​μL of Tc and 100 ​μL of Levo.

In this work, MoS2qds based fluorescent chemo sensor was established for the detection of two different antibiotics namely, Tc and Levo simultaneously. This section describes the physiochemical characterization and optical properties of MoS2qds. Besides this, it was used for the determination of Tc and Levo in the spiked samples of milk, tap water and sewage water. Finally, efforts were made to evaluate the possible mechanism behind the PET process governing the excitation of fluorescence intensity upon adding Levo to the quenched MoS2qds-Tc mixture. Fig. 1(a) shows the Schematic illustration of MoS2qds synthesis and its application as chemo sensor.

UV–Vis absorption spectrum of MoS2qds shows an absorbance maximum at 308 ​nm related to the excitation features of MoS2qds [Fig. 1(b)] [30]. As the MoS2qds show strong quantum confinement effect, it leads to broader band gap energy. The graph between square root of αE (α is absorbance coefficient and E (hυ) is photon energy) and E is shown in Fig. 1(b) inset. The energy band gap of MoS2qds was 3.713 ​eV, higher than bulk MoS2 (1.2 ​eV) and monolayer MoS2 (1.8 ​eV), calculated using Tauc plot [15]. Fluorescence spectra were taken at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 290 to 330 ​nm. The PL spectrum was deconvoluted to find out the exact peak positions as shown in the inset of Fig. 1C. It showed two distinct peaks centred at 399 ​nm and 440 ​nm. The peak at 399 ​nm was from the band edge and the other broad peak at 440 ​nm was due to the surface defects or surface traps that were present on the MoS2qds [31,32]. Moreover, the highest intense emission peak was observed when MoS2qds excited at 310 ​nm [30]. The excitation intensity was increased from excitation wavelength of 290 ​nm–310 ​nm, which is shown by green line in Fig. 1(c). This may be accredited to the hot PL from the k point of the Brillouin zone and the non-uniform size of MoS2qds [30,33]. The quantum yield was calculated at 310 ​nm excitation wavelength and obtained to be 9.65%. The details of the quantum yield calculation using slope method are given in Fig. S1. The emission from broad peak is at 478 ​nm.

FTIR spectrum of MoS2qds is shown in Fig. 1(d). There are broad absorption bands at 761 ​cm-1 and 912 ​cm-1. The absorption band at 912 ​cm-1 is associated to S–S bond. The peak at 761 ​cm-1 appeared due to bending of amine (NH2) or NH group of primary and secondary amines. The peaks at 440 ​cm-1 correspond to Mo–S vibrations [34].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image shows the uniform distribution of MoS2qds at 20 ​nm scale bar [Fig. 2 (a)]. The MoS2qds are well dispersed and show a narrow size distribution ranging from 1 to 8 ​nm with an average 6.153 ​± ​0.939 ​nm [Fig. 2(b)]. The polydispersity of qds may be responsible for the red shift in the PL spectra with increasing excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent chemo sensing of Tc and levo
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of Tc on the fluorescence intensity of MoS2qds was monitored by fluorescence measurements [Fig. 3 (a and b)]. The addition of Tc to MoS2qds solution leads to the decrease in fluorescence intensity with a right shift in the cumulative peak position of MoS2qds (inset of Fig. 3(a)). Upon increasing the concentration of Tc from 0.05 to 5 ​mM, the fluorescence intensity was quenched gradually up to 5 ​mM [Fig. 3(a)]. The detection range was obtained as 0.05–5 ​mM with lower detection of 50 ​μM. Fig. 3(b) shows the calibration plot between fluorescence intensity vs concentration of Tc. Fig. 3 (b) inset shows the linearity plot of Tc obtained between 0.05 and 2.0 ​mM. Here the cumulative peak at one concentration was deconvoluted and found it was the combination of two peaks 410 ​nm and 450 ​nm [inset of Fig. 3(a)]. The band edge and surface trap peaks obtained in pristine MoS2qds were shifted towards higher wavelength side with decrease in fluorescence intensity.

Further, the behaviour of the sensor was investigated by adding Levo to the MoS2qds-Tc mixture. The concentration of Tc was fixed at 5 ​mM [Fig. 3(c)]. Upon increasing the concentration of Levo from 10 ​μM to 1 ​mM, the fluorescence intensity got enhanced with a gradual shift in the peak towards higher wavelengths (Em. 458 ​nm). The detection range of Levo is in the range of 10 ​μM to 1 ​mM with lower detection of 10 ​μM. Again, the cumulative peak was deconvoluted and found two peaks as shown in inset of Fig. 3(c). The two peaks observed were at 450 ​nm and 496 ​nm. Here the peak at 450 ​nm was due to the interaction between Levo and MoS2qds-Tc mixture and the other peak at 496 ​nm represent the emission spectra from quinolone derivative of Levo [35,36]. The enhancement in the peak at 450 ​nm is much higher representing the successful interaction between Levo and MoS2qds-Tc complex and hence sensing. The possible mechanism is explained in Scheme (Fig. 6). The calibration plot was plotted between the fluorescence intensity vs concentration of Levo [Fig. 3(d)]. Fig. 3 (d) inset shows the linearity plot of Levo showed from 10 μM to 0.8 ​μM.

The effect of Tc on fluorescence quenching efficiency; and further addition of Levo on fluorescence recovery of the mixture were investigated. The fluorescence quenching efficiency and fluorescence recovery were calculated using equation given below.
Quenching
Efficiency
( % )
=
Fo − F
Fo
Recovery
Efficiency
( % )
=
( Fr − F )
/
( Fo − F )
where, Fo and F are the fluorescence intensity without and with Tc, respectively. Fr is the recovered fluorescence intensity of MoS2 qds after adding Levo into the cocktail (MoS2qds-Tc-Levo).
The quenching efficiency of Tc is 95% while the recovery efficiency of Levo is 283%.

Fig. 4 (i) shows FTIR spectrum of Levo (i), IR peaks at 1001 ​cm-1 (COOH; monomeric stretching and bonding), 1533 ​cm-1 (NH2 deformation) and 1647 ​cm-1 ​(C=O stretch), corresponding to functional group of Levo structure. A broad IR band in the region of 3647–2985 ​cm-1 (-NH and –OH stretching) was observed. Similar IR peaks corresponding to Levo are reported in literature [37]. However, after the addition of Levo into MoS2qds-Tc solution (ii), the peak at 1001 ​cm-1 (-COOH) was shifted to 1023 ​cm-1. Other major peaks at 3200 ​cm-1 (-OH vibration), 1396 ​cm-1 (SO2 anti-symmetric stretch), 1535 ​cm-1 (NH2) and 2935 ​cm-1 (aliphatic –CH) appeared. The results show that Levo is interacting with MoS2qds-Tc mixture.

The time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy (TRFS) studies were carried out to check the quenching type. Fig. S2 shows the Fluorescence life-time of (a) MoS2 qds (b) MoS2 qds-Tc (c) MoS2 qds-Tc-Levo as calculated using time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy (TRFS) by 375 ​nm laser. The life-time of MoS2qds was obtained as 8.4 ns, while with Tc as the quencher, it reduced to 7.8 ns. Further, adding of Levo to MoS2 qds-Tc, the life-time changed to 11.25 ns.

The most common types of fluorescence quenching can be classified as static quenching, dynamic quenching and inner filter effect (IFE) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [38]. The interaction of MoS2qds with Tc is dynamic in nature [Fig. 5
(b)] as calculated by fluorescence life-time studies (Fig. S2) and confirmed by other works in the literature [15,38,39].

Further, the quenched fluorescence intensity of MoS2qds-Tc was recovered after the addition of Levo. Levo contains carboxylic group (CG) (act as a donor group); in free form CG of Levo directly involves in electronic cloud of the chromophore via resonance effect. Therefore, it does not act as the donor group. However, when interacted with MoS2qds-Tc mixture, the CG rotates perpendicular to the ring and do not involve directly in electronic cloud of chromophore ring. Now this CG acts as the donor group, [Fig. 5 (d)] which is involved in the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process. PET takes place from Levo to surface traps (S.T) of the MoS2qds-Tc mixture. This result in radiative electron – hole recombination and enhanced the fluorescence intensity [Fig. 5 (c)]. Here, Levo ignited the fluorescence intensity of MoS2qds-Tc mixture. In summary, the enhancement of fluorescence intensity of MoS2qds-Tc complex after the addition of Levo is due to the interaction between the MoS2qds-Tc mixture. Levo contributed majorly and the fluorescence properties of Levo further increased the cumulative peak intensity.

Spiked samples of milk, sewage and tap water were prepared by adding antibiotics concentration of 1.5 ​mM Tc and 125 ​μM Levo to the sample as shown in Fig. 6. A similar phenomenon of quenching in the presence of Tc and reappearance of fluorescence in addition of Levo was observed. However, in Fig. 6 (b) and (c) an additional peak was obtained in the MoS2qds-Tc graph around 530 ​nm. This could be due to the presence of other species (metal ions, Ca etc.) and contaminants in sewage and tap water.

The interference study was done with different antibiotics including erythromycin, ofloxacin, gentamycin and ampicillin having fixed concentration of 100 ​mM and other complex interferents of milk (casein, citric acid, urea and BSA) studied with fixed concentration of TC and Levo (1 ​mM). Fig. 7 shows the interference results. It was found that only in the presence of Tc the fluorescence was quenched and the fluorescence intensity increases only in the presence of Levo while other analytes have no significant effect on the fluorescence. These results confirmed the specific interaction for Tc and Levo only. Thus, to conclude this study, the proposed optical chemo sensor based on MoS2qds shows more response towards Tc and Levo via quenching the fluorescence intensity of Tc and thereby igniting the fluorescence intensity of Levo by the mixture.

In summary, MoS2qds were synthesized using hydrothermal method taking sodium molybdate and l-Cysteine as the precursor compounds. The synthesized MoS2qds showed bright blue fluorescence when excited in the UV range. The quantum yield of MoS2qds was 9.65%. The MoS2qd was used as an optical chemo sensor with high selectivity towards Tc and Levo. The fluorescence was quenched when Tc was added to the MoS2qds solution due to dynamic quenching. The fluorescence was ignited when Levo was added to the MoS2qds-Tc mixture. PET process governs the ignition of fluorescence because of the rotation of CG and thereby acting as an electron donor group to the MoS2qds-Tc mixture. The fluorescence intensity quenching efficiency of Tc was 95% and the recovery efficiency of Levo was 283%, which is higher than previously reported work. The lower detection was obtained as 50 ​μM for Tc while for Levo it was10 μM, which is very sensitive. Also, the detection was done in spiked samples of milk and water (sewage and tap) samples. The spiked samples showed the similar effect of quenching with Tc and reappearance of fluorescence after adding Levo to the mixture. Moreover, the different compound present in real samples, as well as other antibiotics does not interfere with the Tc and Levo detection. Also, interference results showed the optical biosensor is specific to Tc and Levo antibiotics. The study showed that the Off-On optical chemo sensor can be easily upgraded to the real sample analysis without any additional modulation. It also suggested that the present method is easily scalable from bench side to bedside. However, efforts should be laid on the development of sensor which is more robust.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to AIRF, JNU for providing the characterization techniques (AFM, TEM and TRFS). The work done here is supported by Department of Biotechnology, New Delhi, India –Indo-Russia project (DBT/IC-2/Indo-Russia/2017–19/02). One of the authors, Smriti Sri is thankful to UGC for providing financial support through SRF. GBVSL is thankful to DST, India funding through women scientist project (SR/WOS-A/PM-108/2016).
